msgs.text:
Turn 23 ‚ÄĒ coefficient-Hensel audit: the square-zero route is too strong I re-read the attached mega_prompt.md, problem_statement.md, and turns_history__q2_candidate_iso.md, using only those attachments. The original target is still the over-G isomorphism problem for the two quotients őďS,őďH, where F=P‚čäG, P is the free G-operator pro-2 group on x0,x1, and rS,rH are the relators from the statement.

The desired conclusion is exactly an isomorphism őďS‚ČÖGőďH, i.e. an isomorphism commuting with the canonical projections to G.

The new conclusion is:

Turn¬†22‚Äôs¬†proposed¬†square-zero¬†coefficient-Hensel¬†lemma¬†is¬†not¬†the¬†right¬†lemma.

This does not produce a new obstruction to őďS‚ČÖGőďH. It means the last gap is sharper than Turn 22 stated: coefficient lifting must be treated as a nonlinear Bockstein/Witt-type finite-level problem, not as ordinary square-zero formal smoothness.

1. Why the Turn 22 lemma is too strong

Turn 22 correctly identified the remaining stress point: Turn 21‚Äôs compactness framework is sound once one uses coefficient-and-degree truncations P/PN,J, but one still has to prove that the finite solution sets SN,J are nonempty for every coefficient quotient.It then proposed a square-zero lifting lemma: for a finite Artinian quotient A of the Ef-coefficient ring and a square-zero ideal a‚äāA, prove surjectivitySN (A)‚ü∂SN (A/a).The proposed reason was that, at a square-zero extension, the new error terms are linear in a, and Nakayama should reduce the obstruction to the residual restricted-Lie complex solved in Turn 18.The problem is exactly the phrase ‚Äúlinear in a‚ÄĚ.The residual Turn 18 mechanism is not purely linear. At even degree 2m, the obstruction quotient isQ2m=L2m/im‚ąā2m‚ąí1‚ČÖLm[2],and it is killed by a square/root transgression

W‚üľW[2].That is the whole point of the residual induction: odd layers are ordinary boundaries, but even layers have a restricted-square quotient, and Turn 18 proves Cm=Lm so every such square quotient is hit.But in a square-zero coefficient extension, if a correction has initial coefficient in a, then its quadratic square contribution has coefficient in a2=0. So the square transgression that kills Lm[2]in the residual problem does not appear in the tangent map.Thus the square-zero tangent map sees only the ordinary boundary‚ąā2m‚ąí1:L2m‚ąí1‚äēL2m‚ąí1‚ÜíL2m,whose cokernel is precisely the dangerous square quotientLm[2].

So Cm=Lm does not imply square-zero formal smoothness. The residual theorem supplies nonlinear square directions, not tangent directions.This is not a minor wording issue. It is the same structural phenomenon seen earlier: fixed or linearized gauges repeatedly missed the correct torsor. Degree 12 already showed that the na√Įve kernel test had rank 9 in an 11-dimensional square quotient, while the full affine kernel supplied the missing directions.Degree 16 was even more explicit: the high torsor alone missed one square direction, and the lower Y0‚Ü¶Y0Y4 base deformation was needed to toggle it.So the Turn 22 square-zero lemma is trying to force a nonlinear Bockstein mechanism into a tangent-space proof. That cannot be the final argument.

2. What remains valid from Turn 21 and Turn 22 The compactness architecture remains correct.

Turn 21 repaired the earlier false claim that the degree-only quotients P/PN are finite. The correct finite approximations truncate both Magnus degree and őõ=Z2 [[G]]-coefficients:MN,J=M/(IN+JM),PN,J=P‚ą©(1+IN+JM).

Then P/PN,J is finite, and one can define finite solution sets SN,J. If all SN,JÓÄ†=‚ąÖ, compactness gives a compatible inverse-limit over-G automorphism carrying rS¬Ī1 to rH, hence an isomorphism őďS‚ČÖGőďH.The idempotent splitting also remains correct. After the Turn 1 abelianized change of variables, the coefficient algebra splits into (1‚ąíE)őõ‚äēE(1‚ąíf)őõ‚äēEfőõ,the first two pieces are unit-controlled, and the only delicate coefficient piece is Efőõ, whose residue is obtained by imposing 2=0 and s=1.The residual Ef,mod2,s=1 tower is still closed: Turn 18 proves Cm=Lm for every m‚Č•5, so every residual even square quotient is hit and every residual odd layer is an ordinary boundary.

So the picture is:compactness¬†framework¬†correct+residual¬†tower¬†closed+coefficient¬†nonemptiness¬†still¬†unproved.3. The correct replacement lemma The correct finite-coefficient lemma should not say that every solution lifts across every square-zero extension.

It should say something weaker and more global:Finite coefficient nonemptiness lemma.For every finite Artinian quotient A of the local Ef-coefficient ring and every Magnus degree N, the full finite lower-plus-high correction problem over A, including section cocycle variables, has at least one solution.This is a nonemptiness statement, not a formal smoothness statement.The reason this distinction matters is that the finite-level solution over A may need to reduce to a different residual solution over A/mA than the one chosen at a previous stage. Degree 16 is the model example: a fixed high gauge saw a codimension-one obstruction, but changing the lower residual base by Y0‚Ü¶Y0Y4 killed it.Thus the coefficient problem must allow retuning of the lower residual base while solving the coefficient layers. It cannot be phrased as ‚Äúlift this fixed residual solution.‚ÄĚ

A better formulation is:BA‚Č§d‚ü∂ n<N‚ąŹTailn(A),where BA‚Č§d is the full finite torsor of lower and high corrections over A, not merely the tangent kernel over a fixed solution. At even degree 2m, the relevant map is not the linear tangent boundary alone, but a coefficient-Bockstein transgressionB2m,A:Cm (A)‚ü∂Q2m(A),whose residue is the known map

W‚Ü¶W[2].

The missing proof is:imB2m,A contains¬†the¬†actual¬†finite¬†coefficient¬†square¬†class¬†for¬†every¬†A,N.

That is the coefficient analogue of the residual theorem Cm=Lm, but it is not identical to it.

4. First coefficient layers to test The first genuinely new coefficient quotients are the two length-two directions of the local ring

Efőõ‚ČÉZ2 [[s]]near(2,s‚ąí1).

So the immediate finite coefficient tests should be:A2=Z2 [[s]]/(4,s‚ąí1),which isolates the 2-Bockstein direction, andAt=Z2 [[s]]/(2,(s‚ąí1)2),which isolates the s‚ąí1 coefficient direction.These are not random cases. They are the two generators of the maximal ideal m=(2,s‚ąí1)in the Ef-local coefficient ring. If both coefficient-Bockstein directions are absorbed by the same lower-plus-high torsor mechanism, then the likely induction over mr/mr+1 becomes plausible. If either produces a square class not movable by the coefficient torsor, that becomes the first serious post-residual obstruction candidate.This is the structural test that closes many cases at once: do not hammer one word tail; test the two coefficient generators of the local ring and the induced Bockstein transgression.

5. Status after Turn 23 The conclusion is no longer ‚Äúthe coefficient-Hensel lemma is ready to write.‚ÄĚ It is:The¬†square-zero¬†formal-smoothness¬†version¬†of¬†coefficient-Hensel¬†is¬†the¬†wrong¬†target.The compactness proof will close once we prove finite coefficient nonemptiness over all Artinian quotients of Efőõ. But that proof must allow nonlinear residual retuning and Bockstein square transgressions. It cannot be reduced to a square-zero tangent surjectivity statement.

So I would not yet state the final theoremőďS‚ČÖGőďH as fully proved. The evidence still points toward isomorphism, because every actual residual obstruction through the full mod-2 tower has collapsed and the non-Ef pieces are unit-controlled. But the coefficient lift remains a real proof obligation.

ESTABLISHED: Turn 22‚Äôs proposed square-zero coefficient-Hensel route is not valid as stated. In a square-zero extension, the nonlinear square transgressions W‚Ü¶W[2]vanish at the tangent level, so Cm=Lm over the residue field does not imply formal smoothness of SN (A)‚ÜíSN (A/a). Turn 21‚Äôs coefficient-and-degree compactness framework remains correct, and Turn 18‚Äôs residual tower remains closed.

GAP: It remains to prove finite coefficient nonemptiness for every Artinian quotient of the Ef-local coefficient ring. This requires a nonlinear coefficient-Bockstein torsor argument, not square-zero tangent surjectivity.

NEXT: Test and formulate the coefficient-Bockstein induction over the local ring Efőõ‚ČÉZ2 [[s]], beginning with the two length-two quotients Z2 [[s]]/(4,s‚ąí1) and Z2 [[s]]/(2,(s‚ąí1)2). If the lower-plus-high torsor absorbs both generators 2 and s‚ąí1, promote this to an induction over mr/mr+1.[TURN23-SQUARE-ZERO-HENSEL-REJECTED]
